storm.summary_layperson: The storm cells that showed up earlier this afternoon have dissipated — radar is now clear, and with almost no atmospheric energy available, the evening risk is light showers at most.
storm.local_effect_blurb: Sound water (68°F) is only 4°F cooler than the air, so the usual Riverside coastal buffer is not meaningfully active tonight.
storm.detail_html:
KOKX radar as of 21:26Z shows no storm cells within detection range — the three cells that were visible 27–29 miles SW at 17:10 EDT have dissipated. CAPE is 30 J/kg (HRRR f01), essentially zero fuel for storm development. The OKX Area Forecast Discussion (issued 19:58Z) notes "tall/skinny CAPE less than 500 J/kg" across the region and places any isolated thunderstorm risk in northeastern New Jersey and the Lower Hudson Valley. No NWS watches, advisories, or warnings are active for Greenwich. Bulk shear is 31.8 kt WSW — high shear is only relevant when instability is present, and tonight it isn't.

Light shower activity is still present in the region: Port Chester / Rye Brook (N1TKS, 4 mi WSW) logged 0.3 mm/hr, and Fort Salonga LI (DW1086, 15 mi ESE) recorded 0.3 mm/hr. These are disorganized shower cells consistent with the NWS "Chance Rain Showers, 50%" forecast. No upstream APRS stations are showing any gust precursor — all nearby sites are reporting near-calm, so there is no approaching gust signature to track.

The coastal stabilization modifier is weak. Sound water is 67.6°F vs. inland air 71.6°F — a delta of 4°F, below the 10°F threshold for the Riverside protective mechanism. The protective_mechanism_active flag is false. With CAPE at 30 J/kg the mechanism's absence doesn't matter much — the low-end instability means any shower drifting through lacks the energy to produce lightning regardless.

What would change this call: a new convective cluster forming or approaching from the NW–W (Westchester, lower Hudson Valley) with reflectivity above 35 dBZ. The OKX AFD mentions MCS potential in NW flow next weekend, not tonight. Monitor radar if scattered showers increase in coverage, but no action needed at this time.

hourly.shift_note: backing to ESE — boundary-layer decoupling in near-calm
SE, Greenwich Point shadow on east shore

wind.tactics_blurb: Drifter conditions all evening — 1–3 kt with direction wandering 140° through the window, no coherent pressure or favored side. Stay center and minimize losses; this is a finesse-sailing night, not a pressure-hunting one.
wind.tactics_detail_html:
Regime B-light: the temperature differential between inland air (71.6°F at KHPN) and Sound water (67.6°F at BRHC3 Bridgeport Harbor) is 4.0°F — just below the 5°F gate required to trigger the sea-breeze thermal engine. Mostly cloudy skies suppressed surface heating further: coastal southerly fraction is 0.28, with zero southerly penetration inland. The synoptic gradient is equally negligible — authoritative station mean just 3.4 kt. With both ingredients absent, tonight is firmly Regime B-light.

Direction coherence is very low: the 26-station ensemble direction agreement is 0.38. RYC and the airports (KBDR, KHPN) show NNW; nearby APRS stations show WNW; central LIS airports (KHVN) show WSW; eastern LIS/RI stations show S/SSE. HRRR forecasts a wander from NNW (346°) at 17:00 EDT, to ESE (119°) at 18:00, SE (138°) at 19:00, then S/SSE (150–172°) through 21:00, SSW (207°) by 22:00 — all below 4 kt. At these speeds, HRRR surface direction is essentially numerical noise in a nearly-windless boundary layer; do not trade individual direction calls. Expect random scatter of 30–60° around any single-hour number.

Cove geography: during the NNW phase (17:00–18:00 EDT), cleanest flow is mid-cove, away from either shore. If the direction does shift toward ESE–SE (18:00–20:00), Greenwich Point / Tod's Point creates a partial wind shadow on the east shore — pressure shifts slightly to the west side of the course. With speeds at 1–3 kt, these differences are tenths of a knot; expect large holes everywhere regardless of position.

Sunset is approximately 20:20 EDT. In Regime B-light there is no thermal collapse — the thermal never fired — but HRRR already forecasts ~1 kt by 20:00. Any race starting after 19:30 EDT faces a significant risk of sub-3 kt conditions before the finish.

The NWS point forecast reads "south wind around 6 mph" — already contradicted by current RYC (NNW 1.4 kt, 10-min avg 2.5 kt) and HRRR sub-4 kt output across all hours. Trust the live observations. This is not a sea-breeze maturation divergence; the forecast text describes a synoptic southerly that never materialized at the surface in western LIS.

data_caveats: Buoy 44040 (WLIS) wind sensor offline — over-water wind unavailable; SST available at 62.1°F (8m depth, obs age 92 min).
EW9544 (Old Greenwich waterfront APRS) stale — last report ~19 days ago, excluded from ensemble.
Direction ensemble coherence very low (agreement 0.38) — regime call is robust but hourly direction numbers are low confidence; treat as illustrative.
